text: Successful endurance athletes typically perform about 80% of training sessions at low intensity (below 2 mM blood lactate) and about 20% at high intensity.
The low-intensity threshold for polarized training corresponds to approximately 2 mM blood lactate concentration.
High-intensity training sessions in elite endurance athletes typically involve interval training at approximately 90% of VO2max.
Elite endurance athletes typically train 10 to 13 sessions per week.
Increasing the proportion of high-intensity interval training in already well-trained athletes does not provide convincing evidence of long-term performance gains.
Endurance athletes self-organize toward a high-volume training approach with careful, distributed application of high-intensity training throughout the training cycle rather than concentrated blocks.
The combination of predominantly low-intensity long-duration training with fewer high-intensity bouts optimizes adaptive signaling and technical mastery at an acceptable stress level.
